exercises.name: Executive Update
exercises.rule: One clear message first. Everything else supports it.
exercises.why: Executives remember one idea per update. Give them yours before they start hunting for it.
exercises.objective: Train opening a short update with one clear main message that organizes every supporting point and lands on what should happen next.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: if the update does not lead with one clear main message that the rest visibly supports and close with an implication or next step, structure score MUST reflect the breach.
exercises.coach_insight: A great update sounds decided from the first sentence — the headline could stand alone if she walked away mid-update. The trap is narrating events in the order they happened, which buries your message under a timeline.
common_failure_modes: Opens with chronology or background instead of the main message
States several competing headlines so no single message organizes the update
Includes supporting details that never tie back to the stated message
Ends on a detail or trails off without an ask or next step
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight whether each supporting point audibly serves the stated headline and whether the closing ask is concrete enough for the listener to act on immediately.
exercises.retry_objective: On the retry, state the one-sentence main message inside the first ten seconds, before any background or supporting detail.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt names a senior or time-pressed audience, a live setting, and stakes attached to the update. Situations must contain competing details so the user is forced to pick one organizing message, support it with two or three points, and close with what happens next. The prompt never dictates that structure — finding it is the exercise.

prompts.text: Your CEO has 90 seconds between board sessions. Update her on a launch that slipped two weeks because a key vendor delivered late — and say what you need from her.
Brief your VP in the elevator before an all-hands: the hiring freeze is lifting, but only for two of your five open roles.
Update the executive team on last night's outage. It was contained in an hour, but two enterprise customers noticed and sales wants talking points.
Your director joins your standup unannounced and asks where the migration stands. It is on track, but one dependency could slip the date.
Give the leadership team a 90-second update on the pilot program: early results are promising, but the sample is small and finance wants a decision.
Update the hospital's chief of staff on the new intake process. Wait times dropped, nurses are complaining, and she decides next week whether it expands.
Brief the school principal on the tutoring program before a parents' meeting: attendance doubled, but funding runs out in six weeks.
Your client sponsor calls for a surprise check-in on the review you are running. You are ahead of schedule, but you found duplicate payments that her own team approved.
Update the investment committee on the portfolio company: revenue beat plan, the CFO resigned Friday, and they meet the founders tomorrow.
Give the nonprofit board a 90-second update on the fundraising campaign: halfway to goal with three weeks left, and the biggest donor has gone quiet.
Brief your GM before she meets the union rep: the new scheduling system works, but overtime complaints are up and the rep has the numbers.
Update the head of sales on the CRM rollout: adoption is at 60 percent, the laggards are her top reps, and the old system dies Friday.

exercises.name: Project Briefing
exercises.rule: Announce the map, then walk it in order.
exercises.why: Listeners relax when they know where a briefing is going — tell them the route, then never leave it.
exercises.objective: Train briefing a group on a project by announcing its shape up front, then using explicit transitions so listeners always know where they are and what connects each part.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: if the briefing does not preview its parts up front and mark each transition audibly, structure score MUST reflect the missing map or dropped signpost.
exercises.coach_insight: A great briefing tells the room how many stops the tour has before it starts, then names each one as you arrive. The trap is silent drift between topics — you know you changed sections, but nobody listening does.
common_failure_modes: Dives into the first topic without previewing the briefing's parts
Switches sections with no audible transition, so parts blur together
Announces a map but then walks it in a different order or drops a part
Buries roles, timeline, and dependencies in one undifferentiated stream of detail
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight whether the walk matches the announced map — same parts, same order — and whether a listener could reconstruct the briefing's skeleton from the transitions alone.
exercises.retry_objective: On the retry, name the parts of the briefing in the first two sentences and open each section with an explicit transition word or phrase.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt names the group being briefed, the setting, and a consequence for the briefing landing badly. Each situation must involve at least three moving parts (scope, timeline, roles, dependencies) so a preview-then-walk structure is genuinely useful. Prompts never instruct the user to signpost — the exercise supplies that behavior.

prompts.text: Brief three new teammates joining the website rebuild Monday: the design is done, the build is half-finished, and testing is theirs. They start this week.
Your team won a client project: redesign their store, launch before the holidays, and a budget that cannot move. Brief the delivery team on scope, timeline, and that constraint.
Brief the city council's staff on the bike-lane pilot before the public hearing: what is being built, in what phases, and who is affected when.
A senior partner inherits the review you have been running: three of five areas checked, one red flag in the contracts, report due Friday. She has two minutes before the client call.
Brief the volunteer coordinators on the food-drive logistics: three sites, two weekends, and the trucks are only available Saturday mornings.
Walk the testing team through the release plan: code locks Friday, two weeks of testing, launch on the first. If the lock date slips, the launch slides a full quarter.
Brief new hospital staff on the discharge project: paper forms become a checklist in the app, it goes live June 1, and from day one no patient leaves without their sign-off.
Visitors tour your group Thursday. Brief everyone on a project you are genuinely working on — work or personal — so each of them could describe it accurately to a guest.
Brief the franchise owners on the loyalty-program rollout: pilot stores, timeline, and what corporate covers versus what they pay for.
Walk the marketing and legal leads through the rebrand project together. Their calendars conflict, and both need to leave knowing the sequence.
Brief the incoming club officers on the conference you are handing them: venue, budget, and the three deadlines that cannot move.
Brief the customer's IT team on the connection plan: your product links to their billing system first, then their reporting tools, and their security review must clear before either step.

exercises.name: Problem Overview
exercises.rule: Name the problem fast. Background only if it earns its place.
exercises.why: Most problem descriptions drown in history. Trains you to define the problem before anyone asks you to hurry up.
exercises.objective: Train stating a problem crisply — what is happening, why it matters, and what is unknown — without solutioning and without background that does not change the listener's understanding.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: if the problem is not clearly named in the opening sentences, or the response drifts into solutions or unearned background, conciseness score MUST reflect the breach.
exercises.coach_insight: A great problem statement sounds like a diagnosis, not a story: what's broken, who it hurts, what's still unknown. The trap is autobiography — walking through how you discovered it — or sneaking a favorite fix in the back door.
common_failure_modes: Spends the opening on history or context before naming the problem itself
Slides into proposing solutions instead of defining the problem
Pads with background that doesn't change the listener's understanding
Lists symptoms without saying why the problem matters now or what's unknown
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight the ratio of problem definition to everything else, and whether any background that survived actually changes what the listener understands.
exercises.retry_objective: On the retry, cut the weakest background detail entirely and state the problem itself within the first fifteen seconds.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt names a listener, a setting, and why the problem matters now. Situations must tempt the user toward history, excuses, or premature fixes so scoping discipline is tested. Prompts ask for the problem only — solutions are explicitly out of bounds or clearly premature.

prompts.text: Your manager asks what is wrong with the checkout flow before her next meeting. Conversion dropped 15 percent, and you get one minute to define the problem, not solve it.
Tell the engineering lead why support tickets doubled this month. He is between interviews and only needs the shape of the problem.
The clinic director asks why patients are missing follow-up appointments. Give her the problem in 60 seconds; the board meets tonight.
Explain to your co-founder why the sales pipeline looks healthy but revenue is flat. She has heard three theories already and trusts none of them.
Brief the dean on why first-year students are dropping the intro course. Enrollment decides the department budget, and she wants the problem, not a fix.
Tell the warehouse manager why the picking error rate spiked. The shift starts in ten minutes and he has to explain it to his crew.
The co-op board asks why the building's energy bill jumped 40 percent. Give the owners the problem plainly before they vote on the retrofit.
Explain to the new CTO why releases take three days here: every change waits on two manual sign-offs and a once-a-week testing slot. She is deciding this week what to fix first.
Tell the PTA why the school fundraiser missed its target. Parents are frustrated, and next year's event depends on naming the real problem.
Brief the restaurant owner on why weekday lunch traffic collapsed. He blames the construction; your data says otherwise.
The client asks why the campaign's cost per lead tripled. You have 90 seconds on the call before their CMO joins.
Explain to the team why onboarding new hires takes six weeks when the plan says two. HR is in the room and already defensive.

exercises.name: Data Explanation
exercises.rule: Say what the number means, not just what it is.
exercises.why: A chart is not an explanation. Trains you to turn evidence into one idea a non-analyst can repeat.
exercises.objective: Train presenting data to a mixed audience by leading with what the evidence means, anchoring it in one concrete comparison, and stating honestly what it does and does not show.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: if the response recites figures without stating what they mean for this audience, or overstates what the data shows, clarity score MUST reflect the breach.
exercises.coach_insight: Great data talk gives the so-what first and lets one comparison carry the number — 'that's every third customer walking out.' The trap is reading figures aloud and hoping meaning arrives on its own.
common_failure_modes: Recites a string of figures without saying what any of them mean
Skips the caveat or split result, overstating what the data shows
Uses analyst jargon on an audience the prompt says is non-expert
Leaves numbers floating with no comparison the listener can picture
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight whether the nuance embedded in the data — the split, caveat, or misleading average — was conveyed in plain language a non-analyst could repeat back correctly.
exercises.retry_objective: On the retry, open with the meaning of the data in plain language and keep at most two numbers, each attached to a concrete comparison.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt names a non-expert or mixed audience, a setting, and a decision or reaction riding on the data. Each situation embeds a nuance (a split result, a caveat, a misleading average) the user must convey without jargon. Prompts describe the data verbally so no visual aid is assumed.

prompts.text: Tell the sales team the quarter's win rate: one deal in four closed, up from one in five — but only on the smaller deals. Half the room stopped reading dashboards long ago.
Explain the A/B test result to your PM: variant B won by two points, but only on mobile. She decides today whether it ships everywhere.
Walk the clinic staff through the patient-satisfaction survey. Scores fell in one area only, and morale is already shaky.
Explain the churn analysis to the founder: customers from one channel leave twice as fast, and he is about to double that channel's budget.
Present the reading-scores data to parents at a school meeting. The average rose, but the gap between groups widened — say both clearly.
Explain your survey to the student council: 70 percent want later library hours, but only 5 percent of students voted. The budget decision is tonight.
Walk your manager through the load-test results: the system held at twice current traffic and failed at three times. Black Friday is projected at 2.8x.
Explain the donor-retention chart to the nonprofit board: first-time donors rarely give again, and the annual gala plan assumes they do.
Present the energy-audit numbers to the building owners: the boiler is 30 percent of the bill, and the retrofit pays for itself in four years.
Explain the injury-rate data to the plant safety committee: incidents fell overall but rose on night shift. The union rep will quote you.
Walk the marketing lead through the attribution report: the ads she loves get clicks, and a channel nobody discusses gets the conversions.
Explain the waitlist data to the daycare's advisory board: demand doubled, but only for infant spots, and the license caps how many infants you can take.

exercises.name: Recommendation Presentation
exercises.rule: Recommend one option. Show the reasoning that survives questions.
exercises.why: A recommendation is only as strong as its weakest unspoken assumption. Trains you to argue for a choice, not just announce it.
exercises.objective: Train presenting a single recommendation supported by evidence the audience can inspect, acknowledging the strongest point for the other option, and closing with the concrete next step.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: if the recommendation is not clearly stated, is supported by assertion instead of evidence, or ignores the obvious counterargument, thinking quality score MUST reflect the breach.
exercises.coach_insight: A great recommendation sounds like a verdict with visible evidence: choose A because of X and Y, even though B wins on Z. The trap is the balanced tour of both options that ends without you ever actually picking one.
common_failure_modes: Walks through both options evenly and never commits to one
Supports the pick with adjectives and confidence instead of inspectable evidence
Ignores or strawmans the strongest argument for the other option
Ends on the reasoning without a concrete next step
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight the reasoning chain itself — whether the evidence offered genuinely supports the pick, and whether the counterargument is answered rather than merely mentioned.
exercises.retry_objective: On the retry, name the strongest argument against your recommendation out loud and answer it before your close.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt names a decision-making audience, a live setting, and a genuine two-sided choice where evidence exists on both sides. Situations must make hedging tempting so committing to one option is the tested behavior. Prompts never reveal which option is correct — there isn't one.

prompts.text: Recommend to your VP which of two vendors gets the contract. Both demo well; the difference is risk, and she will ask what could go wrong.
Tell the leadership team whether to build or buy the analytics tool. Engineering wants to build, finance wants to buy, and you have the analysis.
Recommend to the clinic board whether to extend evening hours. Staff costs are real, missed appointments are real, and you have both sets of numbers.
Present your recommendation to the school board: keep the aging bus fleet one more year or lease new ones now. Either answer costs someone something.
Recommend to the founders whether to raise prices 20 percent. The churn model disagrees with the sales team's gut, and you must say whose evidence wins.
Tell the student housing committee which meal-plan proposal to adopt. Every option angers one group; recommend anyway, with reasons that survive the town hall.
Recommend to your manager whether to sunset the legacy API. Twelve customers still use it, one is the biggest account, and support costs keep climbing.
Present to the town council your recommendation on the intersection: roundabout or signal. Residents pack the room, and the traffic study cuts both ways.
Recommend to the nonprofit director whether to accept the corporate sponsorship. The money doubles the budget; the donor's reputation is mixed.
Tell the IT steering committee whether to delay the ERP go-live. The integrator says ready, your testing says almost, and payroll runs on it in six weeks.
Recommend to your team lead which candidate gets the offer. The stronger coder bombed the collaboration interview, and the team is fragile.
Present your recommendation to the department head: cut the underperforming program now, or give it one more semester with changes.

exercises.name: Closing Summary
exercises.rule: End with what they must remember and do. Nothing else.
exercises.why: The last 30 seconds are the only part everyone hears. Trains you to spend them on the takeaway, not a recap of everything.
exercises.objective: Train closing a longer session by distilling it to the few points worth carrying out of the room and the action attached to them, resisting the urge to re-summarize everything.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: if the close re-walks the whole session, exceeds a handful of takeaways, or ends without a clear implication or action, conciseness score MUST reflect the breach.
exercises.coach_insight: A great close is smaller than the meeting it ends: a few takeaways sharp enough to survive the hallway, then the action, then silence. The trap is the full replay that makes everything said equally forgettable.
common_failure_modes: Re-walks the whole session point by point instead of selecting takeaways
Lists so many takeaways that none stand out
Ends without a clear action, owner, or deadline
Keeps talking after the action sentence, diluting the close
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight the selection judgment — whether what was kept is genuinely the most consequential material from the session — and how cleanly the response actually stops.
exercises.retry_objective: On the retry, cap the close at three takeaways and end on the single action sentence — nothing after it.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt places the user at the end of a longer session with a named audience and a reason the close matters. The described session must contain more material than the close can hold, forcing selection. Prompts imply time pressure — the room is leaving, the next speaker is waiting.

prompts.text: The hour-long planning meeting is ending and the director turns to you: sum us up. Give the room what was decided and who does what by when.
Close the day-long client workshop on fixing their slow customer service. They heard forty slides; leave them with the two things that must survive the car ride home.
You are ending a short talk on a subject you know well. The next speaker is being miked up, and the audience will only retain what you say in these final seconds.
Wrap the outage review for the on-call team: the alert that never fired, the outdated guide, and the expired password — only the alert changes what they do tonight.
Close the parent-teacher meeting on the new grading policy. Some parents came angry; end with what changes for their kids and where questions go.
End the investor update call: growth beat plan, hiring runs behind, and you will not raise money until spring. Close with the single message you want repeated at their partner meeting.
Wrap the volunteer training session: they saw the whole manual, but Saturday only requires them to remember three things.
Close the project demo for stakeholders who arrived late: the new dashboard shipped, exports slipped a week, and you need their scope decision by Friday.
End the sales call with the hospital buying team: a 90-day pilot, two departments, cancel anytime. Summarize the terms so cleanly they can repeat them to the committee you will never meet.
Close the town-hall Q&A on the office relocation. Rumors are ahead of facts; end with what is decided, what is not, and when people hear next.
Wrap your study group's last session before the exam: end with what the group should review tonight and what to let go.
End the budget presentation to the finance committee. After twenty minutes of tables, close with the ask, the deadline, and the cost of waiting.

exercises.name: Audience Translation
exercises.rule: Lead with what this audience cares about. Cut the rest.
exercises.why: Content that impresses one room bores the next. Trains you to re-cut the same material for whoever is actually listening.
exercises.objective: Train presenting material built for one audience to a different one by leading with what the new audience cares about, keeping only the details that matter to them, and framing implications in their terms.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: if the response is a generic replay that ignores the named audience's priorities, vocabulary, or stakes, clarity score MUST reflect the breach.
exercises.coach_insight: Great translation starts from this room's Monday morning — what changes for them — and treats your original material as raw stock, not script. The trap is a polite find-and-replace that swaps words but keeps the old room's priorities.
common_failure_modes: Replays the original version with the old audience's framing intact
Leads with what impressed the previous room, not what this audience risks or gains
Keeps technical detail or jargon the new audience has no use for
Never names this audience's stakes — what changes for them stays implicit
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight the opening seconds — whether this audience's stakes lead — and judge what the speaker cut from the original material, not just what they added.
exercises.retry_objective: On the retry, name what this audience stands to gain or lose in your first sentence, and cut any detail that does not serve it.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt names a specific audience with obvious, distinct priorities and material either supplied by the prompt or drawn from something the user genuinely knows, originally framed for a different room. Situations must make the original, generic version tempting so re-cutting for the listener is the tested behavior. Prompts never tell the user to adapt — the audience's stakes make it necessary.

prompts.text: You just explained the app redesign to your dev team. Now the customer support team wants to hear about it — they take the calls when anything changes.
This morning you explained a hobby or subject you love to a fellow enthusiast. Now present it to a retirement community that invited you to speak over lunch.
You pitched the new menu to your head chef yesterday. Today the restaurant's investors are at the table, and margins are tight.
Tell the parents' association about the new school schedule you already presented to teachers. Pickup times and childcare fill their inboxes.
Present the security review's findings — shared passwords and unencrypted laptops — to the engineering managers. Yesterday the board heard the same findings and asked only about liability.
Walk the warehouse night shift through the new safety protocol you wrote for corporate compliance. They care whether it slows the line.
Explain the hospital's new records system to the nurses who will chart in it twelve hours a day. The demo you have was built for administrators.
Present the quarterly marketing results to the sales team. Impressions and brand lift mean nothing to them; their pipeline is what pays their rent.
Describe the merger integration plan to the acquired company's employees at their first all-hands. Leadership already heard the synergy version.
Present the city's flood-mitigation plan to homeowners in the affected neighborhood. The engineering firm's technical version is the only material you have.
Pitch a subject you know deeply — from work, study, or a hobby — to an investor as an opportunity. This morning you explained the same subject to a fellow specialist.
Brief the union stewards on the automation roadmap the executive team approved. The deck celebrates efficiency; the room is worried about jobs.

exercises.name: Concept Grounding
exercises.rule: Turn every abstraction into something the listener can picture.
exercises.why: Nobody acts on an abstraction. Trains you to give strategy and metrics a shape people can see, weigh, and repeat.
exercises.objective: Train presenting abstract concepts, metrics, or strategy by translating each one into a concrete image, comparison, or day-in-the-life scenario the listener can picture and act on.
exercises.scoring_lens: Constraint: if abstractions are restated or defined with more abstractions instead of translated into concrete, picturable terms, clarity score MUST reflect the breach.
exercises.coach_insight: Great grounding puts a scene where the buzzword was: a Tuesday shift, a phone call, a queue of thirty thousand flagged transactions. The trap is defining one abstraction with another — it sounds clear and lands as nothing.
common_failure_modes: Defines the abstraction with synonyms or more abstractions
Gives a technically correct definition with nothing the listener can picture
Grounds the first concept, then lets later abstractions pass untranslated
Picks an image from the wrong world — a scenario this listener would never live
exercises.scoring_emphasis: Weight whether each concrete translation is both accurate to the concept and fitted to this specific listener's daily reality, not merely vivid.
exercises.retry_objective: On the retry, attach a concrete image, comparison, or scenario to every abstract term within a breath of saying it.
exercises.prompt_rules: Every prompt hands the user something abstract — a strategy phrase, metric, value, or concept — plus a specific listener who needs to grasp what it means for them. Situations must resist mere restatement so translation into tangible terms is forced. Prompts never ask for analogies or examples explicitly — supplying them is the exercise.

prompts.text: Your company's new value is 'radical ownership.' Explain to a new hire on their first day what it means here.
Explain to your 15-year-old cousin what inflation running at 4 percent actually means for the family this year.
The gym's program promises 'progressive overload' — asking a little more of your body each week. A new member asks what that actually means for their Tuesday workout.
Tell the new volunteers what 'food insecurity affects one in eight households here' means for the delivery routes they drive Saturday.
The strategy deck says the company is 'moving up-market.' Explain to the support team what changes about the customers they'll talk to.
Leadership announced 'zero-trust security' — no device or login is trusted automatically. Explain to the sales team what that means for the demo laptops they carry into client offices.
Tell the school board what a 0.4 drop in average GPA looks like inside an actual classroom, before they vote on the tutoring budget.
Your nonprofit's impact report says 'capacity building.' Explain to a first-time donor at the gala what her five-thousand-dollar gift actually does.
The clinic is adopting 'patient-centered care.' Explain to the front-desk staff what changes when the phone rings Monday morning.
The earnings script says 'headwinds compressed margins.' Explain to the all-hands what actually happened, and why bonuses are affected.
Your fraud model has a false-positive rate of 3 percent. Explain to the review team what their queue looks like at a million transactions a day.
The city plan promises 'walkability improvements.' Explain to skeptical shop owners on the affected street what their block looks like in a year.
